You are ONE of N parallel CANDIDATE planners (generate-N-then-judge) for plan-flow goal **G13**. Run in **CANDIDATE MODE**: ground yourself READ-ONLY against the actual repo and the goal's answered Q&A, then RETURN a full candidate task-DAG as a single fenced ```json block. Write NOTHING to any ledger. Emit no status token.

Goal **G13** (milestone M50, `goals` ledger) — "Operability & safety for weighted aggregation + pacing". Read its full description from the ledger. Its five clarifying questions Q51–Q55 are ALL ANSWERED — these answers are BINDING scope decisions:

- **Q51 (priority-class scope):** Item 3(ii)'s "latency/priority class" is scoped to protecting wanbond's OWN telemetry PROBE frames (`frame.KindProbe`) from pacer shedding under overload (so overload cannot induce spurious path-DOWN / failover flaps). Inner-tunnel ICMP prioritization is EXPLICITLY OUT OF SCOPE and must be documented as architecturally infeasible (the WG tunnel payload is opaque `ClassData` to the pacer). ClassControl (WG handshake/keepalive) is already pacing-exempt (D22) — do not re-plan that.
- **Q52 (capacity-sanity guard):** When `policy="weighted"` is selected: if `link_bandwidth` IS declared AND `EngageFraction × per_path_capacity_fps` exceeds the implied line-rate capacity (aggregation mathematically can never engage), that is a HARD config-load FAILURE (fail-fast, refuse to start, actionable error). If `link_bandwidth` is NOT declared, it is a startup WARN only (log + a `wanbond_weighted_capacity_sane` gauge=0), never blocks startup. Use `SizePacingFromBDP`/BDP math for the implied capacity (impliedCapacityFPS = link_bandwidth/(8×avgWireFrameBytes)).
- **Q53 (G2 boundary — Option A):** G13 stays OPERABILITY-ONLY: the capacity-sanity WARN/FAIL guard (item 2), the `wanbond_aggregation_engaged` + offered-load/threshold observability (item 1), and the PROBE priority class (item 3(ii)). The actual `per_path_capacity_fps` AUTO-DERIVE from `link_bandwidth` AND the item-3(a) BDP-sizing DOCS remain G2/Q20's scope — G13 does NOT own them (reference, do not restate). Note: G2's Q20 pacing-sizing is intent-only (M14/M16/M17 empty) but you must NOT fold it into G13.
- **Q54 (metric shape):** Per-peer labels (`labelPeer`, consistent with existing FEC/resequencer per-peer series). Expose: `wanbond_aggregation_engaged{peer}` (bool gauge), `wanbond_offered_load_fps{peer}` gauge, and static `wanbond_aggregation_engage_threshold_fps{peer}` / `wanbond_aggregation_disengage_threshold_fps{peer}` gauges. ALSO emit an INFO engage↔disengage transition log line (parity with liveness up/down transitions). The scheduler already holds this state (`s.aggregating`, `loadRate` EWMA, EngageFraction/DisengageFraction × PerPathCapacity) but exposes none to the metrics Source/Collector.
- **Q55 (acceptance harness):** ALL per-task acceptance is via the `-tags e2e` netns fixture for uniformity (privileged, `just e2e`, end-to-end) — NOT unprivileged unit tests. Note: TestFixtureImpairment (test/e2e/fixture_impairment_test.go, T35) exposes rateMbit/lossPct capping and metrics.Fetch scrapes /metrics; if it cannot yet drive a SUSTAINED overload that flips `wanbond_aggregation_engaged` AND concurrently observe PROBE-frame starvation, an explicit fixture-EXTENSION task must be an up-front dependency of the tasks that need it.

Definition-of-done for the repo (do NOT re-derive): `go test` GREEN plus `just lint` across default+e2e+realhosts tags (every task's gate includes lint). Keep README.md / docs/design.md / docs/install.md in sync where a task changes operator-visible behavior (per AGENTS.md).

Ground against the real code: internal/sched (weighted scheduler, FrameClass, token bucket, aggregation gate state), internal/metrics (Source/Collector, labelPeer/labelPath, Exposition), internal/config (validate, per_path_capacity_fps default 10000, link_bandwidth, SizePacingFromBDP), internal/frame (KindProbe), internal/telemetry (liveness transitions, Prober), test/e2e (netns fixture, TestFixtureImpairment).

Produce a candidate plan whose tasks are fine-grained, correctly sequenced (dependsOn edges), each with a crisp testable `-tags e2e` acceptance criterion, grounded in real files, and COMPLETE for the answered scope (items 1, 2, 3(ii) + docs of the on/off tradeoff and the inner-ICMP infeasibility note; NOT the G2-owned auto-derive/BDP-sizing docs). Return EXACTLY this shape and nothing else:

```json
{
  "milestones": [{ "title": "...", "dependsOn": ["<other milestone title>"] }],
  "tasks": [{ "headline": "...", "description": "...", "acceptance": "...", "suggestedModel": "...", "milestone": "<milestone title>", "dependsOn": ["<other task headline>"], "ledgerRefs": ["goals:G13"] }],
  "rationale": "..."
}
```

End your reply with a `### Session summary` section (role: candidate planner (fable), goal G13, "candidate plan emitted", milestone/task counts).

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.

The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.

- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

14	// requiredMode is the exact permission bits the config file must carry. The file
15	// holds the WireGuard private key and the outer-control PSK, so it must not be
16	// readable by group or others.
17	const requiredMode fs.FileMode = 0o600
18	
19	// Load reads, validates, and returns the configuration at path. It fails fast:
20	// the file must have mode 0600 exactly, must be valid TOML, and must satisfy the
21	// required-field invariants.
22	func Load(path string) (*Config, error) {
23		info, err := os.Stat(path)
24		if err != nil {
25			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config: %w", err)
26		}
27		if perm := info.Mode().Perm(); perm != requiredMode {
28			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: insecure permissions %#o, must be %#o (holds private key and PSK)", path, perm, requiredMode)
29		}
30	
31		data, err := os.ReadFile(path)
32		if err != nil {
33			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config: %w", err)
34		}
35	
36		var c Config
37		dec := toml.NewDecoder(bytes.NewReader(data)).DisallowUnknownFields()
38		if err := dec.Decode(&c); err != nil {
39			var strictErr *toml.StrictMissingError
40			if errors.As(err, &strictErr) {
41				return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: unknown key %s", path, unknownKeys(strictErr))
42			}
43			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
44		}
45		if err := c.normalize(); err != nil {
46			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
47		}
48		if err := c.validate(); err != nil {
49			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
50		}
51		return &c, nil
52	}
53	
54	// unknownKeys renders a StrictMissingError's row list as a comma-separated
55	// list of dotted key paths (e.g. "wireguard.peers.nane"), so a misspelled
56	// TOML key surfaces as a precise, single-line diagnostic instead of the
57	// library's raw multiline dump.
58	func unknownKeys(err *toml.StrictMissingError) string {
59		keys := make([]string, 0, len(err.Errors))
60		for _, e := range err.Errors {
61			keys = append(keys, strings.Join(e.Key(), "."))
62		}
63		return strings.Join(keys, ", ")
64	}

14	// requiredMode is the exact permission bits the config file must carry. The file
15	// holds the WireGuard private key and the outer-control PSK, so it must not be
16	// readable by group or others.
17	const requiredMode fs.FileMode = 0o600
18	
19	// Load reads, validates, and returns the configuration at path. It fails fast:
20	// the file must have mode 0600 exactly, must be valid TOML, and must satisfy the
21	// required-field invariants.
22	func Load(path string) (*Config, error) {
23		info, err := os.Stat(path)
24		if err != nil {
25			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config: %w", err)
26		}
27		if perm := info.Mode().Perm(); perm != requiredMode {
28			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: insecure permissions %#o, must be %#o (holds private key and PSK)", path, perm, requiredMode)
29		}
30	
31		data, err := os.ReadFile(path)
32		if err != nil {
33			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config: %w", err)
34		}
35	
36		var c Config
37		dec := toml.NewDecoder(bytes.NewReader(data)).DisallowUnknownFields()
38		if err := dec.Decode(&c); err != nil {
39			var strictErr *toml.StrictMissingError
40			if errors.As(err, &strictErr) {
41				return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: unknown key %s", path, unknownKeys(strictErr))
42			}
43			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
44		}
45		if err := c.normalize(); err != nil {
46			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
47		}
48		if err := c.validate(); err != nil {
49			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
50		}
51		return &c, nil
52	}
53	
54	// unknownKeys renders a StrictMissingError's row list as a comma-separated
55	// list of dotted key paths (e.g. "wireguard.peers.nane"), so a misspelled
56	// TOML key surfaces as a precise, single-line diagnostic instead of the
57	// library's raw multiline dump.
58	func unknownKeys(err *toml.StrictMissingError) string {
59		keys := make([]string, 0, len(err.Errors))
60		for _, e := range err.Errors {
61			keys = append(keys, strings.Join(e.Key(), "."))
62		}
63		return strings.Join(keys, ", ")
64	}

56	// TOML key surfaces as a precise, single-line diagnostic instead of the

23	// codec's concern: the inner WireGuard keepalive is per-peer, not per-path, so a
24	// dead uplink under a live peer is invisible to WireGuard and must be detected
25	// here.

29		// StateDown means no fresh probe echo has been observed within the detection
30		// threshold; the path is presumed unusable.
31		StateDown PathState = iota
32		// StateUp means the path has recently returned probe echoes.
33		StateUp

40			return "up"

42			return "down"

48	// LivenessConfig holds the up/down detection thresholds. DownAfter is the
49	// silence duration that marks an up path down; UpAfterSuccesses is the number of
50	// consecutive echoes required to bring a down path up. The asymmetry is the
51	// hysteresis: a single lost echo never flaps the path, and a single recovered
52	// echo never prematurely declares it healthy.
53	type LivenessConfig struct {
54		DownAfter        time.Duration
55		UpAfterSuccesses int
56	}
57	
58	// Default per-path liveness detection timing (T13/T37/T39). These are the SINGLE
59	// SOURCE OF TRUTH for probe cadence and up/down hysteresis. The daemon
60	// (internal/device) builds its ProberConfig from them and runs its probe loop at
61	// DefaultProbeInterval; the e2e acceptance table (test/e2e/thresholds.go) derives
62	// its PLiveness* budget from them. Neither can silently diverge from the timing the
63	// daemon actually runs, which is the D16 reconciliation: one place to retune.
64	//
65	//   - DefaultDownAfter is the silence that marks an UP path DOWN.
66	//   - DefaultProbeInterval is the probe emission AND liveness Tick cadence; detection
67	//     latency is therefore bounded by DownAfter + one interval.
68	//   - DefaultUpSuccesses is the consecutive echoes that bring a DOWN path UP.
69	//   - DefaultLossWindow (0) takes the estimator's default trailing window.
70	//
71	// DownAfter spans DefaultProbeInterval*6, so SIX consecutive lost echoes are needed
72	// before a false DOWN — the D13 jitter-safety margin. T39 tightened the pair from
73	// 1500ms/250ms to 1200ms/200ms to WIDEN the P1 3s-recovery margin while KEEPING
74	// that six-lost-echo false-down tolerance exactly (the ratio is unchanged). Detection
75	// latency drops from ~1.75s to ~1.4s worst case, and the receive-path liveness sweep
76	// (bind, D15) makes that detection robust to probe-loop-ticker starvation under load.
77	const (
78		DefaultProbeInterval = 200 * time.Millisecond
79		DefaultDownAfter     = 1200 * time.Millisecond
80		DefaultUpSuccesses   = 3
81		DefaultLossWindow    = 0
82	)

84	// Liveness is the per-path up/down state machine. It is driven by two events:
85	// RecordEcho on every authenticated probe echo, and Tick on a periodic timer.
86	// It starts Down and requires UpAfterSuccesses echoes before declaring Up, so a
87	// path is never reported healthy before it has actually answered.
88	type Liveness struct {
89		cfg   LivenessConfig
90		clock Clock
91		log   log.Logger
92	
93		state      PathState
94		lastGood   time.Time
95		haveGood   bool
96		goodStreak int
97	}
98	
99	// NewLiveness builds a per-path liveness machine. The supplied logger is tagged
100	// with the path name so every transition record carries the per-path field.
101	func NewLiveness(pathName string, cfg LivenessConfig, clock Clock, logger log.Logger) *Liveness {
102		return &Liveness{
103			cfg:   cfg,
104			clock: clock,
105			log:   logger.Path(pathName),
106			state: StateDown,
107		}
108	}
109	
110	// RecordEcho registers a successful, authenticated probe echo as a liveness
111	// heartbeat. Only echoes that are CONSECUTIVE — no more than DownAfter apart —
112	// accumulate toward Up; an echo that arrives after a longer silence starts the
113	// streak over, so UpAfterSuccesses stray echoes spread across arbitrary silence
114	// never bring a Down path Up. Once UpAfterSuccesses consecutive heartbeats
115	// accumulate, a Down path transitions Up.
116	func (l *Liveness) RecordEcho() {
117		now := l.clock.Now()
118		if l.haveGood && now.Sub(l.lastGood) > l.cfg.DownAfter {
119			l.goodStreak = 0 // silence broke the run of consecutive echoes
120		}
121		l.lastGood = now
122		l.haveGood = true
123		l.goodStreak++
124		if l.state == StateDown && l.goodStreak >= l.cfg.UpAfterSuccesses {
125			l.transition(StateUp, now)
126		}
127	}
128	
129	// Tick re-evaluates liveness against the clock. An Up path with no heartbeat for
130	// longer than DownAfter transitions Down; a Down path that has gone silent past
131	// DownAfter has its partial up-streak reset, so recovery requires a fresh run of
132	// UpAfterSuccesses consecutive echoes rather than echoes accumulated before the
133	// silence. Call Tick at least as often as the probe interval so detection latency
134	// stays within DownAfter plus one interval.
135	func (l *Liveness) Tick() {
136		now := l.clock.Now()
137		if !l.haveGood {
138			return
139		}
140		stale := now.Sub(l.lastGood) > l.cfg.DownAfter
141		switch {
142		case l.state == StateUp && stale:
143			l.goodStreak = 0
144			l.transition(StateDown, now)
145		case l.state == StateDown && stale:
146			l.goodStreak = 0
147		}
148	}
149	
150	// State returns the current liveness verdict.
151	func (l *Liveness) State() PathState { return l.state }
152	
153	func (l *Liveness) transition(to PathState, now time.Time) {
154		from := l.state
155		l.state = to
156		l.log.Info("path liveness transition",
157			"from", from.String(),
158			"to", to.String(),
159			"silence_ms", now.Sub(l.lastGood).Milliseconds(),
160		)
161	}

- `WeightedScheduler` (internal/sched/weighted.go) holds all gate state under `s.mu` (`aggregating`, `loadRate`, `cfg.EngageFraction/DisengageFraction × PerPathCapacity`, token buckets) but exposes none of it; `updateGateLocked` flips the gate silently, while liveness (internal/telemetry/liveness.go `transition`) and pacer shedding (`shedLocked`) already log INFO transitions — the parity target for Q54.
- `metrics.Source` (internal/metrics/metrics.go) has per-concern snapshot methods (`Paths/FEC/Reseq/Session/PeerNames`) with a single-peer back-compat rule for `labelPeer`; `Exposition`/`Fetch` (scrape.go) already support e2e assertions.

- Decisive discovery for item 3(ii): PROBE frames NEVER pass `scheduler.Pick` — `emitProbes` writes them directly to each path socket (multipath.go `peerPathState` txBytes comment). The pacer cannot shed them, but it budgets zero headroom for them: a pace sized at link rate lets paced DATA + the probe stream oversubscribe the link, building the standing queue that delays/drops probes past `DownAfter` (1200ms) → the spurious path-DOWN flap Q51 targets. The correct wanbond-visible fix is exempt-but-charged probe accounting, not a new `Pick` class.
- test/e2e: `SetupWithPaths`/`netemArgs` expose `rateMbit`/`lossPct`; there is no sustained calibrated load driver nor concurrent metrics/log sampling helper — the Q55 fixture-extension task is genuinely needed up front.
